title: Obligations, rules & advice
description: Learn to talk about rules, obligations, and giving advice using must, have to, need to, and should. Real-world contexts: workplace, school, safety, and everyday problems.

lessons.title: Must & mustn't — rules and prohibitions
lessons.description: Use must and mustn't to talk about strong rules, laws, and prohibitions.

content.title: Must — strong obligation
content.body:
We use MUST + base verb to say something is necessary or very important.

Examples:
• You must wear a helmet on a bike.
• Students must carry their ID cards.
• I must call my mother today.

MUST shows a strong personal or official rule. It often comes from the speaker or from authority.
content.grammar_note:
must + base verb (no 'to')
I/You/He/She/We/They MUST go

content.title: Mustn't — prohibition
content.body:
We use MUSTN'T + base verb to say something is NOT allowed — it is prohibited.

Examples:
• You mustn't park here. (It's illegal.)
• Students mustn't use phones during exams.
• You mustn't smoke inside the office.

Key difference:
MUST = you are required to do it
MUST'T = you are NOT allowed to do it
content.grammar_note:
mustn't = must not
MUST NOT + base verb

vocabulary.example: This is a company regulation.
Smoking is prohibited here.
Attendance is compulsory.

vocabulary.meaning: money you pay for breaking a rule
vocabulary.example: You will get a fine for speeding.
You need permission to enter.
Parking there is a traffic violation.

sentences: You must show your ID at the gate.
Employees mustn't share passwords.
You must switch off your phone in the cinema.
Visitors mustn't take photographs inside.
We must follow all safety regulations.

content.question: Which sentence uses MUST correctly?
options: You must to wear a helmet.
You must wearing a helmet.
You must wear a helmet.
You musts wear a helmet.
content.correct: You must wear a helmet.
content.explanation: MUST is followed by the base verb with no 'to' and no -ing.

content.question: A sign says 'No Photography'. Which sentence is correct?
options: You must take photographs.
You mustn't take photographs.
You don't must take photographs.
You can't must take photographs.
content.correct: You mustn't take photographs.
content.explanation: MUSTN'T means it is prohibited — not allowed.

content.question: Ravi's manager says: 'All staff ___ wear the company uniform.' What word fits?
options: should
must
would
could
content.correct: must
content.explanation: Must shows a strong official obligation from authority (the manager/company).

content.question: Which is a prohibition (something NOT allowed)?
options: You must carry your badge.
You must arrive before 9 AM.
You mustn't use the emergency exit for normal exits.
You must sign the register.
content.correct: You mustn't use the emergency exit for normal exits.
content.explanation: MUSTN'T indicates prohibition — something that is not allowed.

content.title: First day at a new office — Bangalore
content.setting: Priya is joining a new IT company in Bangalore. Her manager Arjun is explaining the rules on her first day.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Welcome to TechBridge, Priya! I'm glad you joined us.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Thank you, Arjun sir. I'm very excited to start.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Let me explain a few office rules first. You must wear your ID badge at all times on the premises.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Of course. I have it here. What about the working hours?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: You must log in by 9:30 AM. If you're late, you must inform your team lead in advance.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Understood. Are there any rules about lunch breaks?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes — you must take your break in the cafeteria or the break room. You mustn't eat at your workstation.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Oh, I didn't know that. What about mobile phones?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: You can use your phone, but you mustn't make personal calls from the client meeting room. That's strictly prohibited.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Got it. What about the server room? I saw a sign near it.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes — you mustn't enter the server room without permission. It's a security regulation.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'll remember all of this. Thank you for explaining everything so clearly!

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: No problem at all. If you have any doubts, you must ask your team lead or HR. We're always happy to help.

comprehension_questions.question: What must Priya wear at all times in the office?
options: Her uniform
Her ID badge
Safety gloves
A formal jacket

comprehension_questions.question: Where must Priya NOT make personal phone calls?
options: At her desk
In the cafeteria
In the client meeting room
In the break room

options: She gets a fine.
She must call the main office.
She must inform her team lead in advance.
She must not come in.
comprehension_questions.correct: She must inform her team lead in advance.

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake in each sentence.

sentences.wrong: You must to switch off your phone before the meeting.
sentences.correct: You must switch off your phone before the meeting.
sentences.explanation: MUST is followed directly by the base verb — no 'to'.

sentences.wrong: Employees mustn't not bring food into the server room.
sentences.correct: Employees mustn't bring food into the server room.
sentences.explanation: MUSTN'T already means 'must not'. Don't add 'not' again.

sentences.wrong: She musts show her ID before entering.
sentences.correct: She must show her ID before entering.
sentences.explanation: MUST does not change form — never add 's' for third person.

content.scenario: You are visiting a hospital in Chennai. You see a sign that says 'SILENCE — Patients resting'. Your friend starts talking loudly on the phone. What do you say?
options: You can talk quietly here.
You mustn't talk loudly here — patients are resting.
You should probably not talk here maybe.
Talking is not very good here.
content.correct: You mustn't talk loudly here — patients are resting.
content.explanation: MUSTN'T is the right choice for a clear prohibition based on an official sign and a strong reason.

content.instruction: Think about your school, college, or workplace. What rules do people MUST follow? What are they NOT ALLOWED (mustn't) to do? Speak for at least 60 seconds.
content.prompt: Tell me 3 things you MUST do and 3 things you MUSTN'T do at your school, college, or office.
content.example_response: At my college in Chennai, we must wear our ID card every day — security checks at the gate. We must attend at least 75% of classes, otherwise we can't appear for exams. We also must switch off our phones before entering the exam hall. On the other hand, there are some things we mustn't do. We mustn't copy during exams — that's a serious violation and you can be suspended. We mustn't enter the staff room without permission. And we mustn't park our bikes in the main entrance — there's a separate parking area for students.

content.instruction: Look at these signs and explain what each one means using MUST or MUSTN'T. Speak naturally as if explaining to a visitor from another country.
content.prompt: Imagine you are showing a foreign friend around a public place in India. Explain 4 common rules using must and mustn't.
content.example_response: In India, when you visit a temple, you must remove your shoes before you go inside. This is a mark of respect. You mustn't point your feet towards the deity — that's considered very rude. At a metro station in Chennai, you must keep your bag open for security scanning. You mustn't carry sharp objects or liquids above 100 ml. And on the road, drivers must wear seatbelts — if you don't, you will get a fine from the traffic police.

content.instruction: Complete the office notice with MUST or MUSTN'T.
content.text:
OFFICE NOTICE

All staff ___ (1) follow the dress code policy. You ___ (2) wear casual clothes on client meeting days. Employees ___ (3) finish and submit their timesheets by Friday evening. You ___ (4) leave your workstation unlocked when you step away. All visitors ___ (5) sign in at reception before entering the building.

content.title: Lesson 1 complete — Must & mustn't
key_points: MUST + base verb = strong obligation (required to do it)
MUSTN'T + base verb = prohibition (not allowed to do it)
MUST never changes form — no 's' for he/she/it
No 'to' after MUST
Use MUST for official rules, laws, and strong personal obligations
content.next_lesson: Have to & don't have to — obligations

lessons.title: Have to & don't have to — obligations
lessons.description: Learn the difference between have to (external obligation) and don't have to (no obligation needed).

content.title: Have to — external obligation
content.body:
We use HAVE TO when something is necessary because of an outside rule, law, or situation — not because the speaker wants it.

Examples:
• I have to submit the report by Monday. (My boss requires it.)
• She has to wear glasses when she drives. (Doctor's instruction.)
• We had to wait for two hours at the airport. (Past obligation)

Key point: HAVE TO comes from outside — rules, laws, other people.
content.grammar_note:
I/You/We/They HAVE TO + base verb
He/She/It HAS TO + base verb
Past: HAD TO + base verb

content.title: Don't have to — no obligation
content.body:
We use DON'T HAVE TO when something is NOT necessary — you have a choice.

Examples:
• You don't have to wear a tie — it's a casual office.
• She doesn't have to cook today — we're ordering food.
• On Sunday, I don't have to wake up early.

IMPORTANT: DON'T HAVE TO is very different from MUSTN'T!
• Mustn't = prohibited (you cannot do it)
• Don't have to = not necessary (but you can if you want)
content.grammar_note:
I/You/We/They DON'T HAVE TO
He/She/It DOESN'T HAVE TO
Past: DIDN'T HAVE TO

vocabulary.meaning: the last time to finish something
vocabulary.example: The deadline for this project is Friday.
The training is mandatory for all new staff.
The team dinner is optional.
You have to submit the form online.

vocabulary.meaning: to go to / be present at
vocabulary.example: All staff have to attend the safety training.
I had to do overtime to finish the project.

sentences: I have to finish this before five o'clock.
She doesn't have to come to the meeting.
Do you have to work on Saturdays?
We had to wait for the manager.
He doesn't have to wear a uniform.

content.question: Meera works in a hospital. The rules say all staff must have a staff card. Which sentence is correct?
options: Meera don't have to carry her card.
Meera has to carry her staff card.
Meera have to carries her staff card.
Meera doesn't has to carry her card.
content.correct: Meera has to carry her staff card.
content.explanation: HE/SHE uses HAS TO + base verb. This is an external rule.

content.question: The office party is optional. Nobody is forced to go. Which sentence is correct?
options: You mustn't go to the party.
You don't have to go to the party.
You haven't to go to the party.
You not have to go to the party.
content.correct: You don't have to go to the party.
content.explanation: DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary, optional. MUSTN'T would mean it's prohibited.

content.question: Yesterday, Ravi's car was broken, so he ___ take the bus to work.
options: has to
have to
had to
didn't have to
content.correct: had to
content.explanation: Past obligation uses HAD TO. His car broke down, so he was forced to take the bus.

content.question: What is the key difference between MUSTN'T and DON'T HAVE TO?
options: They mean exactly the same thing.
MUSTN'T means not necessary; DON'T HAVE TO means prohibited.
MUSTN'T means prohibited; DON'T HAVE TO means not necessary.
DON'T HAVE TO is more polite than MUSTN'T.
content.correct: MUSTN'T means prohibited; DON'T HAVE TO means not necessary.
content.explanation: This is a very important distinction. MUSTN'T = not allowed. DON'T HAVE TO = not required but you can if you want.

content.title: Planning for a college trip — Mumbai
content.setting: Sonal and Arjun are classmates at a Mumbai college, planning for their annual trip.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Arjun, do we have to pay for the bus for the college trip?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: No! The college is covering transport. We don't have to pay for the bus.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh that's great! What about food?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: We have to bring our own lunch. The college won't provide it.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Okay. And do we have to wear the college uniform?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: No, we don't have to. Casual clothes are fine, but no torn jeans.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: What time do we have to be at the college gate?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: We have to be there by 7 AM sharp. The bus leaves at 7:15.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: That's early! Do we have to carry any documents?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes, everyone has to carry their college ID. Security check on the way back.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Got it. And what about phones — any restrictions?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: We don't have to switch them off. But we mustn't use them during the guided tour at the museum.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Perfect. I'll pack everything tonight. This is going to be so much fun!

options: Food
The bus
Entry tickets
Their ID cards

comprehension_questions.question: What time do students HAVE TO arrive at the gate?
When MUSTN'T students use their phones?
options: During the bus journey
At lunch
During the guided tour at the museum
When they arrive
comprehension_questions.correct: During the guided tour at the museum

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: She have to finish the report by noon.
sentences.correct: She has to finish the report by noon.
sentences.explanation: With HE/SHE/IT use HAS TO, not HAVE TO.

sentences.wrong: You don't have to eat here — it's strictly forbidden.
sentences.correct: You mustn't eat here — it's strictly forbidden.
sentences.explanation: DON'T HAVE TO means not necessary. For prohibition, use MUSTN'T.

sentences.wrong: Yesterday I have to work late because of the deadline.
sentences.correct: Yesterday I had to work late because of the deadline.
sentences.explanation: Past obligation: use HAD TO.

content.scenario: Your friend from Pune wants to visit Singapore as a tourist from India. He asks you: 'Do I need a visa?' You know Indian tourists DO need a visa for Singapore. What do you say?
options: You don't have to get a visa. It's optional.
You mustn't get a visa.
You have to get a visa. Indian tourists need one for Singapore.
You had to get a visa last year.
content.correct: You have to get a visa. Indian tourists need one for Singapore.
content.explanation: HAVE TO is correct because this is an external rule (government law, not your personal preference).

content.instruction: Think about your typical working day or college day. What do you HAVE TO do (obligations)? What DON'T YOU HAVE TO do (things that are optional for you)?
content.prompt: Describe your daily routine using 'have to' and 'don't have to'. Give at least 5 examples.
content.example_response: On a regular weekday, I have to wake up by 6:30 because my office starts at 9 and the commute from my area in Chennai takes about an hour. I have to prepare my own breakfast because my mother leaves early for her work. I don't have to iron my own clothes every day though — I usually do it on Sundays for the whole week. At the office, I have to attend a team standup meeting every morning at 9:15. I don't have to stay till 7 PM — my shift officially ends at 6, but sometimes I have to do overtime if there's a project deadline.

content.instruction: Compare two jobs. For example, a doctor and a software engineer, or a teacher and a shopkeeper. What do they HAVE TO do? What DON'T THEY HAVE TO do?
content.prompt: Compare the obligations of two different jobs using have to and don't have to.
content.example_response: A doctor has to study for many years — at least 5 or 6 years for the basic degree. They have to work very long hours and sometimes on weekends and holidays too. But they don't have to sit at a desk all day — they move around, see patients, do rounds. A software engineer, on the other hand, has to sit in front of a computer for most of the day. They have to meet project deadlines and attend a lot of meetings. But a software engineer usually doesn't have to work on weekends unless there's an emergency — most IT companies follow a proper 5-day week.

content.instruction: Complete this email from an HR department. Use have to, has to, don't have to, or doesn't have to.
content.text:
Dear Team,

Please note the following updates:

1. All employees ___ (1) complete the online data security training by 30th of this month.
2. Part-time staff ___ (2) attend the full-day conference — only the morning session is compulsory for them.
3. The CEO ___ (3) approve all leave requests personally — team leads can now approve up to 3 days.
4. New joiners ___ (4) submit their documents to HR within the first week.
5. Employees who live close to the office ___ (5) use the shuttle service — it is optional.

content.title: Lesson 2 complete — Have to & don't have to
key_points: HAVE TO = external obligation (rules, laws, other people's requirements)
HAS TO = same meaning, used with he/she/it
HAD TO = past obligation
DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary (but you can if you want)
MUSTN'T ≠ DON'T HAVE TO — mustn't = prohibited; don't have to = not required

lessons.title: Need to — necessity
lessons.description: Use need to to talk about what is necessary, and needn't / don't need to for things that are not necessary.

content.title: Need to — something is necessary
content.body:
We use NEED TO + base verb when something is necessary or important.

Examples:
• I need to buy a new phone charger.
• She needs to see a doctor — her fever is very high.
• We need to leave now or we'll miss the train.

NEED TO is similar to HAVE TO but often sounds more personal or urgent.
content.grammar_note:
I/You/We/They NEED TO + base verb
He/She/It NEEDS TO + base verb
Past: NEEDED TO + base verb
Negative: DON'T NEED TO / DOESN'T NEED TO

content.title: Needn't / Don't need to — not necessary
content.body:
Use DON'T NEED TO or NEEDN'T when something is not necessary.

Examples:
• You don't need to bring food — we have enough.
• He needn't worry — the exam is easy.
• They don't need to pay — the event is free.

NEEDN'T is more formal/British. DON'T NEED TO is more common in everyday speech.

Both mean: It's not necessary — you have a choice.
content.grammar_note:
NEEDN'T + base verb (formal)
DON'T NEED TO + base verb (everyday)
NEEDN'T and DON'T NEED TO = same meaning

vocabulary.meaning: needs to be done very soon
vocabulary.example: This is urgent — please reply today.
Water is essential for life.

vocabulary.meaning: a fixed time to meet someone
vocabulary.example: I need to make a doctor's appointment.

vocabulary.meaning: to tell someone about something they might forget
vocabulary.example: Can you remind me to call them?

vocabulary.meaning: to get a new version of something
vocabulary.example: I need to renew my passport.
Please confirm your booking by email.

sentences: I need to renew my driving licence.
She doesn't need to come early.
Do we need to book tickets in advance?
You needn't worry about the bill — I'll pay.
He needed to rest after the long journey.

content.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: She need to submit the form today.
She needs to submits the form today.
She needs to submit the form today.
She needing to submit the form today.
content.correct: She needs to submit the form today.
content.explanation: With HE/SHE/IT: NEEDS TO + base verb (no 's' on the base verb).

content.question: The conference is free. Nobody has to pay. Which is correct?
options: You need to pay to enter.
You don't need to pay to enter.
You mustn't pay to enter.
You needn't to pay to enter.
content.correct: You don't need to pay to enter.
content.explanation: DON'T NEED TO = not necessary. 'Needn't to' is wrong — NEEDN'T is followed directly by the base verb.

content.question: Priya's passport expires next month. She is planning to travel in 3 months. What should she do?
options: She doesn't need to renew it — 3 months is fine.
She needs to renew her passport soon.
She needn't renew her passport because it's expensive.
She mustn't renew her passport before travelling.
content.correct: She needs to renew her passport soon.
content.explanation: NEEDS TO is correct because renewing the passport is necessary before international travel.

content.question: Last week, Arjun was very sick. ___ see a doctor. Which is correct?
options: He needs to
He need to
He needed to
He don't need to
content.correct: He needed to
content.explanation: Past necessity: NEEDED TO + base verb.

content.title: Preparing for a job interview — Hyderabad
content.setting: Kavya is going for her first job interview at a company in Hyderabad. Her older sister Meera is giving her advice.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Meera, my interview is tomorrow and I'm very nervous. What do I need to do?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: First, you need to research the company. Know what they do and their main products.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I've done that already. What about documents? What do I need to carry?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You need to carry multiple copies of your CV and your mark sheets. Don't forget your Aadhar and PAN card.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Do I need to bring my original certificates?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You don't need to bring originals for the first round usually. Photocopies are fine. But carry them just in case.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What should I wear? Do I need to wear formal clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Yes, you need to dress professionally. A formal shirt and trousers or a saree — don't wear jeans.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: How early do I need to reach?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Try to reach at least 15 minutes early. You don't need to arrive an hour early — that's too much.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What if they ask me about my salary expectations?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You need to give a specific range. Don't just say 'whatever you give'. Research the market rate first.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: This is really helpful. You needn't have given me so many tips — but I'm glad you did!

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You're my sister! You'll do great. Just be confident and honest.

comprehension_questions.question: What does Kavya need to research before the interview?
options: Salary rates
The company
Interview questions only
Her mark sheets

comprehension_questions.question: Does Kavya need to bring original certificates for the first round?
options: Yes, originals are essential.
No, she doesn't need to — photocopies are fine.
Yes, but only one original is needed.
No, she needn't bring any documents.
comprehension_questions.correct: No, she doesn't need to — photocopies are fine.

comprehension_questions.question: How early does Kavya need to arrive?
options: 1 hour early
30 minutes early
15 minutes early
On time exactly

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: You needn't to worry about it.
sentences.correct: You needn't worry about it.
sentences.explanation: NEEDN'T is followed directly by the base verb — not 'to'.

sentences.wrong: She need to book the tickets now.
sentences.correct: She needs to book the tickets now.
sentences.explanation: HE/SHE/IT: NEEDS TO + base verb.

sentences.wrong: I didn't needed to pay — it was free.
sentences.correct: I didn't need to pay — it was free.
sentences.explanation: Past negative: DIDN'T NEED TO + base verb (not 'didn't needed').

content.scenario: Your colleague Ravi tells you he is worried about the team presentation tomorrow. He has prepared very well and knows all the content. What do you say to reassure him?
options: You need to worry — it's a very important presentation.
You don't need to worry — you've prepared really well.
You mustn't worry — presentations are prohibited.
You needed to worry more about this.
content.correct: You don't need to worry — you've prepared really well.
content.explanation: DON'T NEED TO is the right reassurance — the worrying is not necessary given his preparation.

content.instruction: Think about something you are currently planning — a trip, an exam, a project, or an event. What do you need to do to prepare for it?
content.prompt: Talk about your upcoming plans and what you need to do to prepare. Use 'need to', 'needs to', and 'don't need to'.
content.example_response: I'm planning to travel to Ooty next month with my friends from college. There are several things I need to do before we go. First, I need to book the bus tickets — they fill up quickly, especially during the weekend. I also need to pack warm clothes because Ooty can be very cold, even in October. One friend needs to arrange the hotel because she's the one with a credit card. We don't need to book every single activity in advance — most things there, like the botanical garden and the lake, you can just walk in. I don't need to exchange currency because we're staying within India. I just need to make sure I have enough cash.

content.instruction: Give advice to a friend who is visiting India for the first time. What does she need to know and do? What doesn't she need to worry about?
content.prompt: Your foreign friend Lily is visiting India for the first time. What does she need to do before and during her trip?
content.example_response: Lily, welcome to India! There are a few things you need to know before you come. You need to get a tourist visa — you can apply online through the e-visa website. You also need to exchange some money — Indian rupees — because not all small shops accept cards. You need to download a translation app because some signs in smaller cities are only in the local language. You don't need to worry too much about food — there are vegetarian options everywhere in India. You also don't need to dress very formally, but you need to carry a light scarf or shawl when visiting temples. And you definitely don't need to book every single day — leave some time to explore freely!

content.instruction: Complete this to-do list email with the correct form of need to.
content.text:
Hi Ravi,

Before the client visit on Thursday:

1. We ___ (1) prepare the conference room — make sure it's clean and the projector works.
2. You ___ (2) send the agenda to all attendees by Tuesday morning.
3. Sonal ___ (3) prepare the product demo — she said she will do it tonight.
4. You ___ (4) print copies of the presentation for everyone in the room.
5. The catering team is handling all food, so we ___ (5) order anything ourselves.

content.title: Lesson 3 complete — Need to
key_points: NEED TO + base verb = something is necessary
NEEDS TO = he/she/it (third person singular)
NEEDED TO = past necessity
DON'T NEED TO / NEEDN'T = not necessary (you have a choice)
NEEDN'T is more formal; DON'T NEED TO is more common in everyday English
No 'to' after NEEDN'T: 'You needn't worry' (NOT 'needn't to worry')

lessons.title: Asking for and giving advice
lessons.description: Use should, shouldn't, ought to, Why don't you, and If I were you to ask for and give confident advice.

content.title: Giving advice with SHOULD and SHOULDN'T
content.body:
We use SHOULD to give advice — to say what we think is the right or best thing to do.

Examples:
• You should drink more water.
• She shouldn't eat so much sugar — it's not good for her.
• You should apply for that job — you're perfect for it.

Asking for advice:
• What should I do?
• Should I talk to him about it?
• Do you think I should quit my job?
content.grammar_note:
SHOULD + base verb (positive advice)
SHOULDN'T + base verb (negative advice)
No change for he/she/it

content.title: More ways to give advice
content.body:
There are several natural ways to give advice in English:

1. If I were you, I would... (strong, personal advice)
   → If I were you, I would talk to your manager.

2. Why don't you...? (friendly suggestion)
   → Why don't you try a different route to work?

3. You ought to... (similar to should, slightly formal)
   → You ought to see a doctor.

4. Have you thought about...? (softer suggestion)
   → Have you thought about working from home on Fridays?

5. It might be a good idea to... (polite, gentle advice)
   → It might be a good idea to book early.
content.grammar_note: All of these are followed by base verb (or -ing after 'about')

vocabulary.example: I'm very stressed about the exam.
You look exhausted — you should rest.
You should improve your communication skills.

vocabulary.meaning: to deal with a difficult situation
vocabulary.example: It's hard to cope with so much pressure.
You should consider all your options.

vocabulary.meaning: to try to find or get
vocabulary.example: You ought to seek medical advice.

sentences: If I were you, I would speak to the doctor.
You shouldn't drink so much coffee — it's not good for you.
Why don't you take a short break and come back to it?
You ought to save some money every month.
Have you thought about asking for a transfer?

content.question: Your friend says she is very tired every day. What is the best advice?
options: You must sleep more — it's a rule.
You should get more sleep and cut down on late nights.
You needn't sleep at all.
You had to sleep early yesterday.
content.correct: You should get more sleep and cut down on late nights.
content.explanation: SHOULD is used for advice and recommendations — what you think is the best thing for the person.

content.question: Arjun is thinking of changing his career. He asks: 'What do you think I should do?' Which is a good, natural reply?
options: You must change your career immediately.
If I were you, I would research a few different options before deciding.
You had to think about it.
You needn't to do anything.
content.correct: If I were you, I would research a few different options before deciding.
content.explanation: 'If I were you, I would...' is a natural and strong way to give personal advice.

content.question: Meera is asking for advice about her health. She looks pale and tired. What do you say?
options: You ought to see a doctor soon.
You mustn't see a doctor.
You don't need to see a doctor ever.
Why don't seeing a doctor?
content.correct: You ought to see a doctor soon.
content.explanation: OUGHT TO gives advice similarly to SHOULD — it's correct and natural here.

content.question: Which sentence is asking for advice?
options: I should talk to my boss.
You should not worry so much.
What do you think I should do about this situation?
If I were you, I would stay calm.
content.correct: What do you think I should do about this situation?
content.explanation: This is a question asking for someone else's advice. The other sentences are giving advice.

content.instruction: Complete with SHOULD or SHOULDN'T.

sentences.text: You ___ skip breakfast — it's the most important meal of the day.
If you want to improve your English, you ___ practise speaking every day.
She ___ spend so much money on clothes — she's trying to save.
You ___ apply for that scholarship — you meet all the criteria.

content.instruction: Complete the advice using the given phrase.

sentences.text: ___ you, I would talk to HR about the problem. (If I were)
___ you join a gym? It might help with the stress. (Why don't)
It might be a good ___ to start earlier tomorrow to avoid traffic. (idea)

content.instruction: Arrange the words to make a correct advice sentence.
content.answer: If I were you, I would start earlier.

content.instruction: Arrange the words to make a question asking for advice.
content.answer: What do you think I should do?

content.instruction: Match the problem with the best advice.

pairs.left: I have a headache.
pairs.right: You should drink water and rest.

pairs.left: My internet is very slow.
pairs.right: Why don't you restart the router?

pairs.left: I'm very stressed at work.
pairs.right: You ought to speak to your manager about your workload.

pairs.left: I want to improve my English.
pairs.right: If I were you, I would practise speaking every day.

pairs.left: I always miss the morning bus.
pairs.right: You should set an earlier alarm.

content.title: Advice between friends — Chennai
content.setting: Priya and Meera are good friends from Chennai. Priya is having some problems at her new job.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Meera, I don't know what to do. My new manager is very difficult to work with.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: What's the problem exactly? What is he doing?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: He gives me too much work and then criticises everything I do. I feel so stressed.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Oh that sounds terrible. Have you thought about speaking to him directly about how you feel?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm scared to. What if he gets angry?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: If I were you, I would still talk to him. You should be calm and professional — explain the workload issue clearly.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: And if he doesn't listen?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Then you ought to go to HR. That's what they are there for. You shouldn't suffer in silence.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: You're right. Should I write things down as evidence?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Yes, absolutely! You should keep a record of everything — emails, feedback, dates. That's very important.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: What if nothing changes? Should I look for another job?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Why don't you give it a month? Try talking to the manager and HR first. You shouldn't make a big decision when you're so stressed.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: That's very sensible advice. Thank you, Meera. I feel better already.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Anytime. You should take care of yourself — that's the most important thing.

comprehension_questions.question: What is Priya's problem at her new job?
options: She doesn't like the work.
Her manager is difficult and gives too much work.
She is not paid well enough.
She doesn't like her colleagues.
comprehension_questions.correct: Her manager is difficult and gives too much work.

comprehension_questions.question: What does Meera say Priya should do if the manager doesn't listen?
options: Quit immediately
Go to HR
Find a new job
Call the police

comprehension_questions.question: What does Meera advise Priya to keep a record of?
options: Her salary
Her daily tasks
Emails, feedback, and dates
The manager's schedule

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: You should to talk to a doctor about it.
sentences.correct: You should talk to a doctor about it.
sentences.explanation: SHOULD + base verb, no 'to'.

sentences.wrong: If I were you, I would to consider taking a break.
sentences.correct: If I were you, I would consider taking a break.
sentences.explanation: WOULD + base verb, no 'to'.

sentences.wrong: Why don't you to apply for the scholarship?
sentences.correct: Why don't you apply for the scholarship?
sentences.explanation: WHY DON'T YOU + base verb, no 'to'.

content.scenario: Your younger cousin Rahul has failed his university entrance exam. He is very upset. He asks you what he should do. What is the most supportive and practical advice?
options: You must pass next time — failure is not allowed.
If I were you, I would take a short break, then make a study plan and prepare again. One failure doesn't define you.
You shouldn't have failed. You need to study harder.
There is nothing you can do. Maybe try a different country.
content.correct: If I were you, I would take a short break, then make a study plan and prepare again. One failure doesn't define you.
content.explanation: This combines empathy with practical, actionable advice — the ideal way to support someone.

content.instruction: A friend asks you for advice. They say: 'I eat a lot of junk food, I never exercise, and I sleep very late every night. I always feel tired and I'm gaining weight. What should I do?' Give them your best advice.
content.prompt: Give advice to your friend about their unhealthy lifestyle. Use should, shouldn't, why don't you, if I were you, and ought to.
content.example_response: First, I think you should cut down on junk food gradually — don't try to stop completely overnight. Why don't you start cooking simple meals at home? Even rice and dal is much better than eating chips and biscuits all day. You shouldn't eat heavy food very late at night — that's one reason you're gaining weight. If I were you, I would also start walking for just 20 to 30 minutes every day. You don't have to go to a gym right away. You ought to fix your sleep routine too — try to sleep before midnight. Your body needs proper rest to have energy. And you should drink at least 8 glasses of water a day. Small changes add up — don't feel overwhelmed!

content.instruction: Think about a problem you have (or invent a realistic one). Explain the problem and then give yourself advice — what should you do?
content.prompt: Describe a real or imagined problem and give advice to yourself using should, shouldn't, why don't you, and if I were you.
content.example_response: I have a problem with time management. I spend too much time on my phone in the evenings, and then I go to sleep late. In the morning, I'm tired and I can't concentrate properly at work. What should I do? Well, I think I should put my phone on silent from 9 PM onwards. Why don't I use that time to read a book instead? If I were advising a friend, I would say: you shouldn't check social media right before sleeping — it keeps your brain active. I ought to create a proper evening routine — maybe light stretching, a cup of chamomile tea, and reading. I think making small changes like this could really improve my energy and focus.

content.instruction: Complete this advice column response. Use should, shouldn't, if I were you, why don't you, or ought to.
content.text:
Dear Advice Column,

My reply to Priya:

I understand how you feel. First, you ___ (1) keep this stress inside — it's not healthy. You ___ (2) speak to your manager calmly and explain that the workload is too much for one person. ___ (3) you keep a diary of all the tasks you're given and how long they take? This is useful evidence. ___ (4) I were you, I ___ (5) also speak to your HR department — they can mediate between you and your manager. Remember, you ___ (6) give up easily — most workplace problems can be solved with clear communication.

content.title: Lesson 4 complete — Asking for and giving advice
key_points: SHOULD + base verb = advice (positive)
SHOULDN'T + base verb = advice (negative)
If I were you, I would... = strong personal advice
Why don't you...? = friendly suggestion
You ought to... = advice (slightly formal)
Have you thought about...? = gentle, softer suggestion
To ask for advice: 'What should I do?' / 'Do you think I should...?'

lessons.title: Rules at work and school
lessons.description: Practise using all obligation and prohibition language in real contexts: workplace and school settings.

content.title: Obligation language — review and comparison
content.body:
Here is a summary of the obligation words you have learned:

STRONG obligation:
• Must (official rule, from authority)
• Have to (external rule, law)

NO obligation:
• Don't have to (not necessary)
• Don't need to / needn't (not necessary)

PROHIBITION:
• Mustn't (not allowed — prohibited)

ADVICE:
• Should (recommended)
• Shouldn't (not recommended)

Using the right word makes your English sound natural and accurate.
content.grammar_note:
must/mustn't — strong
have to/don't have to — external
need to/needn't — necessity
should/shouldn't — advice

vocabulary.meaning: a trial time at the start of a job
vocabulary.example: During your probation period, you must follow all company policies.
Client information is confidential — you mustn't share it.
You should be punctual for all meetings.
All employees must follow the code of conduct.

vocabulary.meaning: a meeting to evaluate your work
vocabulary.example: You have to attend your quarterly performance review.

vocabulary.meaning: unwanted behaviour that makes people uncomfortable
vocabulary.example: Harassment in the workplace is strictly prohibited.
If you are late three times, you will get detention.
Attendance of 75% is compulsory.

vocabulary.meaning: temporary removal from school for breaking rules
vocabulary.example: Cheating in exams can lead to suspension.

vocabulary.meaning: to hand in work to a teacher
vocabulary.example: You must submit your assignments on time.
Bullying results in disciplinary action.
Extracurricular activities are optional but encouraged.

sentences: All employees must follow the company's code of conduct.
Students mustn't use their phones during class.
You have to complete the safety training before starting.
Attendance is compulsory — you don't have to attend extra classes though.
Client information is confidential — you mustn't share it with anyone.

content.question: A new employee's contract says: 'All staff are required to attend the Monday briefing.' Which sentence expresses this correctly?
options: Staff should attend the Monday briefing.
Staff must attend the Monday briefing.
Staff don't have to attend the Monday briefing.
Staff needn't attend the Monday briefing.
content.correct: Staff must attend the Monday briefing.
content.explanation: 'Required to' means it is a strong obligation — MUST is the correct modal here.

content.question: A school notice says: 'Extra coaching classes on Saturday are available but voluntary.' Which is correct?
options: Students must attend Saturday classes.
Students mustn't attend Saturday classes.
Students don't have to attend Saturday classes.
Students have to attend Saturday classes.
content.correct: Students don't have to attend Saturday classes.
content.explanation: Voluntary = optional = don't have to attend.

content.question: Your company's data policy says: 'Employees are strictly forbidden from accessing client data outside office hours.' Which is correct?
options: Employees don't have to access client data outside office hours.
Employees should not access client data outside office hours.
Employees mustn't access client data outside office hours.
Employees needn't access client data outside office hours.
content.correct: Employees mustn't access client data outside office hours.
content.explanation: Strictly forbidden = prohibition = MUSTN'T.

content.question: A colleague says: 'I notice you look very tired. Maybe you need to sleep more and cut down on late-night work.' This is...
options: A rule
A prohibition
Advice
An external obligation
content.correct: Advice
content.explanation: 'Maybe you need to' and 'cut down' are suggestions and advice, not rules or obligations.

content.instruction: Choose the correct option: must, mustn't, have to, don't have to, should.

sentences.text: You ___ share your password with colleagues — it's a security risk. (prohibited)
All students ___ register for exams two weeks in advance. (compulsory)
The gym in the office is free — you ___ pay to use it. (not necessary)
You ___ read the company handbook — it's useful, though not required. (advice)

content.instruction: Fill in the school rule using the correct modal verb.

sentences.text: Students ___ arrive by 8 AM. Latecomers will be marked absent.
You ___ bring your books every day. Everything is provided digitally.
Students ___ bully or intimidate other students. This leads to immediate suspension.
You ___ revise your notes every day — it will help you a lot in exams.

content.instruction: Arrange the words to make a workplace rule.
content.answer: Employees mustn't share confidential information outside the office.

content.instruction: Arrange the words to make a school rule.
content.answer: All students have to wear the school uniform to school.

content.instruction: Match the rule to the correct modal verb category.

pairs.left: Smoking is forbidden on the premises.
pairs.right: mustn't

pairs.left: Wearing a helmet is optional — but advisable.
pairs.right: should / don't have to

pairs.left: All new staff must complete onboarding.
pairs.right: must / have to

pairs.left: You can join the lunch club if you want — it's free.
pairs.right: don't have to

pairs.left: You really ought to get your car serviced.
pairs.right: should / ought to

content.title: New student orientation — Delhi
content.setting: Ravi is a new student at a university in Delhi. He is meeting the student coordinator Kavya during orientation week.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Welcome to the university, Ravi! Are you settling in okay?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Yes, thank you! I have a few questions about the rules here, if you don't mind.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Of course — that's what I'm here for. What would you like to know?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Do I have to attend every single lecture? What if I'm sick?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: You must maintain 75% attendance overall. If you're sick, you should submit a medical certificate to the department office.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: And what about assignments? What's the deadline policy?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: You must submit all assignments on time. Late submissions are penalised. You should always check the submission dates on the student portal.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: Is there a dress code? Do I have to wear formal clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: There's no strict uniform, but you should dress appropriately. You mustn't wear very torn or offensive clothing on campus.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: What about the hostel? Are there curfew rules?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Hostel students must return by 10 PM on weekdays. On weekends, the gate closes at 11 PM. You mustn't stay out without prior permission from the warden.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: That's quite strict. What happens if someone breaks the rules?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Repeated violations result in disciplinary action. You don't have to worry if it's a genuine emergency — just inform the warden.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: This is all very helpful. Thank you, Kavya.

comprehension_questions.question: What percentage of attendance must Ravi maintain?
What should Ravi submit if he is absent due to illness?
options: An email to the class representative
A medical certificate to the department office
A formal letter to the Vice-Chancellor
Nothing — absence is not recorded
comprehension_questions.correct: A medical certificate to the department office

comprehension_questions.question: What time must hostel students return on weekdays?
options: 9 PM
10 PM
11 PM
Midnight

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake in the rule.

sentences.wrong: You don't have to smoke inside the building — it's strictly prohibited.
sentences.correct: You mustn't smoke inside the building — it's strictly prohibited.
sentences.explanation: Prohibited = MUSTN'T. DON'T HAVE TO means not necessary, not prohibited.

sentences.wrong: Students must to carry their ID cards at all times.
sentences.correct: Students must carry their ID cards at all times.
sentences.explanation: MUST + base verb directly — no 'to'.

sentences.wrong: She doesn't needs to attend the optional workshop.
sentences.correct: She doesn't need to attend the optional workshop.
sentences.explanation: DOESN'T NEED TO — 'need' stays in base form after 'doesn't'.

content.scenario: You are the manager of a small company in Bangalore. You need to write a set of rules for your team. One rule is that all client calls must be recorded for quality purposes. How do you write this rule?
options: All client calls should probably be recorded if you want.
All client calls don't have to be recorded.
All client calls must be recorded for quality purposes.
All client calls needn't be recorded — it's optional.
content.correct: All client calls must be recorded for quality purposes.
content.explanation: This is a mandatory company rule — MUST is the correct and professional choice.

content.instruction: Think about your school or college (or remember it). What were the rules? Use MUST, MUSTN'T, HAVE TO, DON'T HAVE TO, and SHOULD to describe at least 6 rules.
content.prompt: Describe the rules at your school or college. What were students required to do? What were they not allowed to do?
content.example_response: At my school in Chennai, we had many rules. We had to wear the school uniform every day — boys had to wear white shirt and navy trousers, and girls had to wear the school salwar kameez. We mustn't come late without a valid reason — three late entries meant a call to your parents. We also had to stand up when a teacher entered the classroom. Mobile phones were not allowed on campus — we mustn't bring them at all. However, we didn't have to stay back for all extracurricular activities — they were optional. We should always address teachers respectfully, that wasn't exactly a written rule but it was expected. And we mustn't cheat in exams — getting caught meant you failed that paper automatically.

content.instruction: Imagine you are the HR manager of a company. You are giving a welcome talk to new employees. Explain the 6 most important rules of your company.
content.prompt: Role-play as an HR manager explaining key company rules to new staff. Use must, mustn't, have to, don't have to, and should.
content.example_response: Welcome to BrightWave Solutions! I want to walk you through our key company policies so you start on the right foot. First, you must wear your employee ID badge whenever you are on the office premises — this is for everyone's security. You should arrive on time for all meetings — punctuality is very important to our culture. You must maintain confidentiality about our client projects — you mustn't discuss client details outside the office or on social media. Now, in terms of flexible policies — you don't have to work from the office every day if your manager has approved a hybrid arrangement. You should, however, be available on video calls during core hours from 10 AM to 4 PM. Finally, harassment of any kind is strictly prohibited — if you experience or witness any issue, you must report it to HR immediately.

content.instruction: Complete this school handbook extract using the correct modal verbs.
content.text:
STUDENT HANDBOOK — KEY RULES

1. All students ___ (1) wear the school uniform on all working days.
2. Students ___ (2) use mobile phones during class hours. Phones found during class will be confiscated.
3. Students who are unwell ___ (3) to inform their class teacher by 8 AM.
4. Attendance at weekly assemblies is mandatory — students ___ (4) skip assembly without medical leave.
5. Extracurricular clubs are optional — students ___ (5) join, but participation is encouraged.
6. Students ___ (6) submit projects on time. Late submissions will receive a 10% penalty.

content.title: Lesson 5 complete — Rules at work and school
key_points: MUST = strong official rule (from authority/law)
MUSTN'T = prohibition (strictly not allowed)
HAVE TO = external obligation (required by outside rules)
DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary (optional)
SHOULD = advice or recommendation
Context matters: read the situation carefully to choose the right word

lessons.title: Safety and warning signs
lessons.description: Read and write safety instructions, warning signs, and emergency notices using the language of obligation and prohibition.

content.title: Safety instruction language
content.body:
Safety signs and instructions use strong, clear language. Common patterns:

• IMPERATIVE (command): 'Wear protective equipment.'
• MUST / MUST NOT: 'You must wear a hard hat.'
• DO NOT: 'Do not enter without permission.' (= You must not enter)
• PROHIBITED / FORBIDDEN: 'Photography is prohibited.'
• WARNING: 'Caution — wet floor.'
• IN CASE OF EMERGENCY: 'In case of fire, do not use the lift.'

All of these express OBLIGATION or PROHIBITION in safety contexts.
content.grammar_note:
DO NOT = MUST NOT = prohibited
ALWAYS = must, you are required to
NEVER = mustn't, prohibited

vocabulary.example: Wet floors are a hazard — be careful.
Caution: falling objects.

vocabulary.meaning: to leave a building quickly in an emergency
vocabulary.example: You must evacuate the building immediately.

vocabulary.meaning: the place where people gather after evacuation
vocabulary.example: Meet at the assembly point near the main gate.
This chemical is highly flammable — keep away from heat.

vocabulary.meaning: basic medical help before a doctor arrives
vocabulary.example: The first aid kit is near the entrance.

content.title: Common warning signs and their meanings

signs.meaning: You mustn't go in. Entry is prohibited.
You must wear a hard hat here.
Use only in emergency. You mustn't block this exit.
Be careful — the floor is slippery. Walk slowly.
Very dangerous electricity — do not touch.
Come here in case of fire or emergency.

sentences: You must wear protective equipment in this area.
Do not leave your bags unattended at any time.
In case of fire, you must evacuate immediately and go to the assembly point.
You mustn't block the emergency exit — keep it clear at all times.
Caution: this surface is slippery when wet.

content.question: A sign in a factory says: 'HARD HATS MUST BE WORN IN THIS AREA'. What does this mean?
options: Hard hats are optional here.
Workers should wear hard hats if they want to.
Workers must wear hard hats — it is compulsory.
Hard hats are not needed here.
content.correct: Workers must wear hard hats — it is compulsory.
content.explanation: MUST BE WORN = it is required/mandatory — workers have no choice.

content.question: A sign says 'DO NOT TOUCH — HIGH VOLTAGE'. Which sentence expresses this rule correctly?
options: You don't have to touch the wires.
You mustn't touch the wires — it's extremely dangerous.
You should try not to touch the wires.
You might not want to touch the wires.
content.correct: You mustn't touch the wires — it's extremely dangerous.
content.explanation: DO NOT = MUST NOT = MUSTN'T — a strong prohibition for safety reasons.

content.question: The building's fire safety notice says: 'In case of fire, evacuate using the stairs. Do not use the lift.' Which explains this correctly?
options: You should probably use the stairs in a fire.
You must evacuate using the stairs and mustn't use the lift.
You can use the lift if you prefer during a fire.
You don't have to use the stairs — the lift is fine too.
content.correct: You must evacuate using the stairs and mustn't use the lift.
content.explanation: In fire emergencies: stairs = must use; lift = mustn't use. Both are strong obligations.

content.question: A 'CAUTION: WET FLOOR' sign means...
options: You are prohibited from walking here.
You must run through this area.
Be careful — the floor is slippery and you should walk slowly.
The floor is always wet — this is normal.
content.correct: Be careful — the floor is slippery and you should walk slowly.
content.explanation: CAUTION is a warning — it advises care, not prohibition. SHOULD walk slowly is the right response.

content.instruction: Write the safety instruction using MUST or MUSTN'T.

sentences.text: Workers ___ wear gloves when handling chemicals.
You ___ prop open the fire door — it must remain closed.
All staff ___ attend the annual fire drill.
Visitors ___ enter the laboratory without a staff escort.

Sign: ALWAYS WEAR SEATBELT. → Passengers ___ wear their seatbelt.
Sign: DO NOT LITTER. → You ___ drop litter here.
Sign: REPORT ALL INJURIES. → All injuries ___ be reported to the supervisor.

content.answer: You mustn't block the emergency exit.

content.title: Fire safety training — IT company, Pune
content.setting: A fire safety officer is conducting a quick training session for staff at a software company in Pune.

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: Good morning, everyone. I'm going to take you through some important fire safety procedures today.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: How often should we do fire drills?

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: You must do a fire drill at least twice a year. It's a legal requirement. Today's session counts as one.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: What do we do if we hear the fire alarm?

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: You must stop what you're doing immediately. You mustn't use the lifts — always use the stairs. Leave your belongings and evacuate calmly.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Where do we go after we evacuate?

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: You must go to the fire assembly point — it's the open area near the main gate. You mustn't go back inside until you receive clearance from the fire department.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: What about the fire extinguishers? Can we use them?

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: You can — but only for small, contained fires. You should never approach a large fire. You must call 101 — that's the fire emergency number in India.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Are there any things we should NOT do during an evacuation?

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: Yes — you mustn't run or push others. You mustn't use your phone during evacuation. And you absolutely mustn't re-enter the building without the all-clear signal.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: What about confidential documents — should we take them?

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: No — you mustn't delay your evacuation for any reason, including documents. Your life is more important than any paperwork.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: This is very clear. Thank you for the training.

dialogue.speaker: Safety Officer
dialogue.text: Remember — you should review these procedures regularly. Safety is everyone's responsibility.

comprehension_questions.question: How often must the company do a fire drill?
options: Once a year
Twice a year
Every month
Every three months

comprehension_questions.question: Where must staff go after evacuating?
options: The nearest road
The fire assembly point near the main gate
The nearest coffee shop
Back to their desk after 5 minutes
comprehension_questions.correct: The fire assembly point near the main gate

comprehension_questions.question: Why mustn't staff take confidential documents during evacuation?
options: Because documents are too heavy.
Because they might lose the documents.
Because life is more important than any paperwork.
Because the documents are stored digitally.
comprehension_questions.correct: Because life is more important than any paperwork.

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake in the safety instruction.

sentences.wrong: Workers don't have to wear safety goggles in the lab. (The sign says: SAFETY GOGGLES MUST BE WORN)
sentences.correct: Workers must wear safety goggles in the lab.
sentences.explanation: The sign says it's mandatory — MUST is required, not don't have to.

sentences.wrong: In case of fire, you should to use the emergency exit.
sentences.correct: In case of fire, you must use the emergency exit.
sentences.explanation: 1. No 'to' after should. 2. In an emergency, MUST is stronger and more appropriate than SHOULD.

sentences.wrong: You mustn't to block the fire exit.
sentences.correct: You mustn't block the fire exit.
sentences.explanation: MUSTN'T + base verb directly — no 'to'.

content.scenario: You work in a hospital in Chennai. A visitor comes in and starts smoking near the entrance. There is a clear 'NO SMOKING' sign. What do you say?
options: You should maybe not smoke here if possible.
You don't have to smoke here.
You mustn't smoke on hospital premises. Please put it out or go outside the gate.
Smoking is a bit discouraged around here.
content.correct: You mustn't smoke on hospital premises. Please put it out or go outside the gate.
content.explanation: MUSTN'T is the right word for a clear prohibition, especially in a health-sensitive area like a hospital.

content.instruction: Describe the safety rules at a place you know — your home, school, office, gym, or a public place like a metro or cinema. What are people required to do? What are they prohibited from doing?
content.prompt: Explain safety rules at a familiar place using must, mustn't, have to, and should.
content.example_response: I want to talk about the safety rules at Chennai Metro stations, which I use regularly. First, you must pass through security check before entering the platform area — all bags go through the scanner and you walk through a metal detector. You mustn't carry any sharp objects, large bottles of liquid, or flammable material — these are prohibited for everyone's safety. On the platform, you must stand behind the yellow line until the train arrives. You mustn't cross the yellow line even if the train is delayed — it's a major safety rule. Inside the train, you should hold the handrails if you're standing. You mustn't eat or drink inside the train — you'll get a fine. There are clearly marked emergency buttons inside each coach, and you should use them only in genuine emergencies. I think these rules make metro travel very safe and organised.

content.instruction: Imagine a fire alarm goes off in your building. Talk through what people must do, mustn't do, and should do step by step.
content.prompt: Describe the emergency evacuation procedure for a fire, as if you are the safety warden explaining it to the team.
content.example_response: Attention everyone — the fire alarm has just gone off. Please stay calm and listen carefully. First, you must stop all your work immediately and close your laptops. You mustn't panic or run — walk quickly and calmly to the nearest emergency exit. You must use the stairs only — you mustn't use the lift under any circumstances during a fire evacuation. Please don't go back for your personal belongings — your safety is more important. Once you're outside the building, you must go to our fire assembly point, which is the open parking area near the main gate. Please do not block the entrance — fire vehicles must have clear access. Team leaders should take attendance and report any missing persons to me immediately. You mustn't go back inside the building until the fire department gives the all-clear signal. Thank you for your cooperation.

content.instruction: Complete this safety notice with MUST, MUSTN'T, SHOULD, or DON'T HAVE TO.
content.text:
LABORATORY SAFETY NOTICE

1. All visitors ___ (1) sign in at the reception before entering the lab.
2. You ___ (2) touch any chemicals without wearing gloves — this is extremely dangerous.
3. Lab coats are provided — you ___ (3) bring your own, but you ___ (4) wear one at all times inside the lab.
4. You ___ (5) report any spillages or accidents to the lab supervisor immediately.
5. Eating and drinking in the lab is ___ (6) not allowed — you must go to the break room.

content.title: Lesson 6 complete — Safety and warning signs
key_points: Safety signs use MUST (required), MUSTN'T (prohibited), and SHOULD (advised)
DO NOT = MUST NOT = MUSTN'T — all express prohibition
CAUTION / WARNING = advice to be careful (should be careful)
In emergencies, MUST is used for critical required actions
Always use the correct modal to match the level of obligation

lessons.title: Advice for common problems
lessons.description: Give and receive advice for real-life problems: travel, health, money, and study — using all A2 obligation and advice language.

content.title: Giving advice — a full toolkit
content.body:
By now you know many ways to give advice in English. Here they all are together:

Strong advice:
• You should/shouldn't...
• You ought to...
• You must/mustn't... (very strong)

Suggestions:
• Why don't you...?
• Have you thought about...?
• What about...?
• How about...?

Personal advice:
• If I were you, I would...

Gentle advice:
• It might be a good idea to...
• You might want to consider...
content.grammar_note:
After WHY DON'T YOU / HOW ABOUT: use base verb
After HAVE YOU THOUGHT ABOUT / WHAT ABOUT: use -ing verb
After IF I WERE YOU, I WOULD: use base verb

vocabulary.meaning: a plan for how to spend money
vocabulary.example: You should create a monthly budget.
Stop procrastinating — start studying now!

vocabulary.meaning: feeling sad because you miss home
vocabulary.example: Many students feel homesick in their first year.

vocabulary.meaning: having too much to deal with
vocabulary.example: I feel overwhelmed with so many assignments.

vocabulary.meaning: to discuss to reach an agreement
vocabulary.example: You should negotiate a better salary.

vocabulary.meaning: to decide what is most important
vocabulary.example: You need to prioritise your tasks.

content.title: Common problem areas — Indian learner contexts
content.content:
Here are typical problem areas where advice is given or asked for:

🏥 HEALTH: headache, stress, lack of sleep, weight, diet
💰 MONEY: overspending, saving, salary, loans
✈️ TRAVEL: visa, packing, delays, being lost
📚 STUDY: exams, procrastination, concentration, motivation
💼 WORK: conflicts with colleagues, workload, career choices
🏠 HOME: managing responsibilities, living alone for the first time

Knowing the vocabulary for each area helps you give relevant, helpful advice.

sentences: If I were you, I would see a doctor straight away.
Have you thought about setting a monthly budget?
Why don't you make a study timetable and stick to it?
You should prioritise your most important tasks first.
It might be a good idea to talk to someone you trust about this.

content.question: Your friend says he always runs out of money by the 20th of every month. What is the best advice?
options: You must not spend any money at all.
You should create a monthly budget and track your expenses.
You shouldn't have a salary.
Why don't you ask your parents for money every month?
content.correct: You should create a monthly budget and track your expenses.
content.explanation: SHOULD is the right advice modal here. The suggestion is practical and constructive.

content.question: A student is very stressed before exams and can't sleep. What advice is most appropriate?
options: You must pass the exam.
You shouldn't sleep at all.
If I were you, I would try relaxation techniques and avoid studying very late at night.
You don't have to take the exam.
content.correct: If I were you, I would try relaxation techniques and avoid studying very late at night.
content.explanation: This is empathetic, practical advice using IF I WERE YOU — appropriate for a personal problem.

content.question: Which sentence uses 'Have you thought about...' correctly?
options: Have you thought about to apply for the job?
Have you thought about applying for the job?
Have you thought about apply for the job?
Have you thought about you apply for the job?
content.correct: Have you thought about applying for the job?
content.explanation: After 'Have you thought about', use the -ing form of the verb.

content.question: Which response to 'What should I do about my bad back?' sounds most natural?
options: You must not have a back.
You ought to see a physiotherapist and avoid sitting for long periods without a break.
You don't need to have a back problem.
You have to buy a new chair immediately.
content.correct: You ought to see a physiotherapist and avoid sitting for long periods without a break.
content.explanation: OUGHT TO gives sensible, natural advice. The answer is both practical and specific.

_____ you talk to your professor about the extension? (Why don't)
If I _____ you, I _____ start saving early. (were/would)

You ___ leave your luggage unattended at the airport — it's a security risk.

content.title: Travel advice — Planning a trip to Kerala
content.setting: Sonal from Pune is planning her first solo trip to Kerala. She is asking her friend Meera, who has been there before, for advice.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Meera, I'm finally going to Kerala next month! I'm so excited but also a bit nervous. What should I know?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Oh, Kerala is absolutely beautiful! Okay, first — you should book your houseboat in Alleppey well in advance. They fill up really fast.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Good tip! What about the best time to travel? Should I be worried about the monsoon?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: If I were you, I wouldn't go in June or July — that's peak monsoon season. October to February is the best time.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: I'm going in October, so that should be fine. What should I pack?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You should carry light cotton clothes — it can be quite humid. And you ought to carry a light rain jacket just in case.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: What about money? Do I need a lot of cash?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Have you thought about carrying a mix of cash and cards? Many smaller places in rural areas don't accept cards.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh, I didn't think of that. Are there any cultural rules I should follow?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Yes! When visiting temples, you must remove your shoes outside. You should dress modestly — don't wear shorts to temples. And you mustn't eat non-vegetarian food inside some of the more traditional temple complexes.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: That's really useful to know. What about transport?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You don't have to rent a car — the state buses are excellent and very affordable. But if you want flexibility, why don't you hire a driver for a day or two?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Great idea! Any safety tips?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: You should always share your itinerary with a family member back home. And you ought to keep photocopies of your important documents.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: This is so helpful, Meera. I'm feeling much more confident now!

options: October
