content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake in the advice.

sentences.wrong: Have you thought about to take a break from social media?
sentences.correct: Have you thought about taking a break from social media?
sentences.explanation: After 'Have you thought about', use the -ing form: TAKING, not TO TAKE.

sentences.wrong: What about you talk to your professor about an extension?
sentences.correct: What about talking to your professor about an extension?
sentences.explanation: After 'What about', use the -ing form: TALKING, not YOU TALK.

sentences.wrong: If I were you, I would to save more money each month.
sentences.correct: If I were you, I would save more money each month.
sentences.explanation: After 'I would', use the base verb directly — no 'to'.

content.scenario: Your younger brother has just started his first job. He tells you he is spending his entire salary every month and has no savings. He is worried about the future. What is the most complete and helpful advice?
options: You must save 100% of your salary every month.
You don't have to save money — you're young.
If I were you, I would save at least 20% of my salary every month. Have you thought about opening a fixed deposit? You should also track your monthly expenses so you know where your money is going.
Why don't you ask for a salary increase?
content.correct: If I were you, I would save at least 20% of my salary every month. Have you thought about opening a fixed deposit? You should also track your monthly expenses so you know where your money is going.
content.explanation: This is the most complete advice — it uses multiple advice structures and gives specific, practical guidance.

content.instruction: A friend comes to you with this problem: 'I have an important exam in two weeks and I haven't started studying at all. I don't know where to begin and I'm very scared.' Give them a full piece of advice — at least 5 sentences.
content.prompt: Give advice for an exam preparation problem using should, shouldn't, why don't you, have you thought about, and if I were you.
content.example_response: Okay, first of all — don't panic! Two weeks is actually enough time if you plan carefully. If I were you, I would start today, not tomorrow. Begin by making a simple study timetable — divide your syllabus into sections and assign one or two topics to each day. You shouldn't try to study everything in one day — that doesn't work and just makes you more stressed. Why don't you start with the topics you find easiest? That will give you confidence to tackle the harder ones. Have you thought about joining a study group with classmates? Explaining things to each other really helps you understand better. You should take short breaks every 45 minutes — study, then a 10-minute break. And you shouldn't stay up all night studying the day before the exam — sleep is very important for memory. You can do this!

content.instruction: Imagine someone asks you: 'I want to improve my English. What should I do?' Give them the most complete and useful advice you can, from your own experience.
content.prompt: Give detailed advice on how to improve English, using all the advice language you know.
content.example_response: Improving your English is absolutely possible with the right approach! First, you should use English every day, not just when studying. Have you thought about changing your phone's language to English? It's a small change but it really helps. If I were you, I would watch English movies and shows with English subtitles — not your mother tongue subtitles — because that trains your brain to process English directly. You should also read every day — even 15 minutes of reading a book, article, or news story in English is very effective. Why don't you keep a vocabulary notebook and write down 5 new words every day? You ought to practise speaking too — even if you don't have a partner, you can speak to yourself, record yourself, and listen back. You shouldn't be embarrassed about making mistakes — every mistake is a chance to learn. And you should be patient with yourself — language learning takes time but every small step counts.

content.instruction: Complete this advice column response. Use the advice phrases in brackets.
content.text:
Dear Reader,

Thank you for writing in about your money problems. Here is my advice:

___ (1) you tried using a budgeting app? It can show you exactly where your money goes each month. ___ (2) I were you, I ___ (3) set a savings goal — even putting aside ₹500 per month is a good start. You ___ (4) spend money on things you don't need — before you buy something, ask yourself: do I really need this? ___ (5) you thought about cooking at home more often instead of ordering food? That alone can save you ₹3,000-4,000 a month. You ___ (6) worry — these are habits that can be changed with practice.

content.title: Lesson 7 complete — Advice for common problems
key_points: SHOULD/SHOULDN'T = most common advice language
IF I WERE YOU, I WOULD = strong personal advice
WHY DON'T YOU + base verb = friendly suggestion
HAVE YOU THOUGHT ABOUT + -ing = gentle suggestion
WHAT ABOUT / HOW ABOUT + -ing = casual suggestion
OUGHT TO = formal advice, similar to SHOULD

lessons.title: Unit 7 Review — Obligations, rules & advice
lessons.description: Review and practise all grammar and vocabulary from Unit 7: must, mustn't, have to, don't have to, need to, and all advice language.

content.title: Unit 7 — Complete grammar review
content.body:
Here is everything you have learned in Unit 7:

1. MUST + base verb = strong obligation (official rule)
2. MUSTN'T + base verb = prohibition (not allowed)
3. HAVE TO + base verb = external obligation
4. HAS TO = he/she/it (third person)
5. HAD TO = past obligation
6. DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary (optional)
7. NEED TO = something is necessary
8. DON'T NEED TO / NEEDN'T = not necessary
9. SHOULD/SHOULDN'T = advice
10. IF I WERE YOU, I WOULD = personal advice
11. WHY DON'T YOU + base = suggestion
12. HAVE YOU THOUGHT ABOUT + -ing = suggestion
13. OUGHT TO = advice (formal)

content.title: Key distinction — Mustn't vs Don't have to
content.body:
This is the most important distinction in Unit 7!

MUSTN'T = NOT ALLOWED (prohibited)
• You mustn't park here. → Parking is forbidden.

DON'T HAVE TO = NOT NECESSARY (optional)
• You don't have to park here. → You have other options — park here or somewhere else.

Test yourself:
→ 'You ___ wear a uniform at my company.' (No uniform rule — it's casual)
→ Answer: DON'T HAVE TO

→ 'You ___ share your password.' (Security rule — forbidden)
→ Answer: MUSTN'T

vocabulary.example: All staff must follow company regulations.
Attendance is compulsory.
Photography is prohibited.
Wet floors are a hazard.
You must evacuate immediately.
You should prioritise your health.
The training is mandatory.
You must submit by the deadline.

sentences: You must wear a seatbelt in a car — it's the law.
She doesn't have to come to the office on Fridays.
If I were you, I would speak to the manager directly.
You mustn't share confidential information with anyone outside the company.
Why don't you try making a weekly study plan?

content.question: Which sentence is a PROHIBITION?
options: You should wash your hands before eating.
You don't have to finish everything on your plate.
You mustn't use your phone while driving.
You need to book a table in advance.
content.correct: You mustn't use your phone while driving.
content.explanation: MUSTN'T = prohibition. The other options are advice, no obligation, and necessity.

content.question: Which expresses NO OBLIGATION (something is not necessary)?
options: You must bring your ID.
You mustn't enter without permission.
You don't have to book — it's a walk-in restaurant.
You should arrive on time.
content.correct: You don't have to book — it's a walk-in restaurant.
content.explanation: DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary, optional.

content.question: Ravi had a terrible journey yesterday — his train was cancelled and he had to wait for 4 hours. Which is CORRECT?
options: He has to wait for 4 hours.
He have to wait for 4 hours.
He had to wait for 4 hours.
He must wait for 4 hours.
content.correct: He had to wait for 4 hours.
content.explanation: Past obligation: HAD TO + base verb.

content.question: Which is the most natural advice for someone who is very stressed about exams?
options: You must stop being stressed.
You ought to take regular breaks and avoid studying late at night.
You don't have to take exams.
You mustn't feel stressed.
content.correct: You ought to take regular breaks and avoid studying late at night.
content.explanation: OUGHT TO gives gentle, practical advice. The other options use incorrect modals for the context.

content.instruction: Choose the best modal verb for each context.

sentences.text: The sign says NO ENTRY. You ___ go in there.
The seminar is free and open to all. You ___ register — just come.
The doctor said I ___ take this medicine twice a day.
You look exhausted. You ___ rest for a few days.
She didn't have a car, so she ___ take the bus to the interview.

You ___ ___ to worry about the cost — dinner is on me tonight. (needn't)
Kavya ___ attend the extra class. It's optional. (doesn't have to)
The company policy says you ___ use personal email for work purposes. (mustn't)

content.answer: If I were you, I would save monthly.

content.instruction: Arrange the words into a correct rule.
content.answer: You must wear a hard hat in this area.

content.title: Unit 7 review dialogue — Job interview preparation
content.setting: Kavya is asking her father for advice about an important job interview at a Bangalore software company.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Appa, I have my final interview at TechCore tomorrow. I'm so nervous. What do I do?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: First, you mustn't panic. You've worked hard for this and you're well prepared.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Should I wear formal clothes or is business casual okay?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: You should definitely wear formal clothes for a final interview. First impressions matter a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What time do I have to be there?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: You should arrive at least 15 minutes early. You mustn't be late — that's a bad start.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What if they ask me about my salary expectations?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: If I were you, I would research the market salary range first. Then give a specific but flexible range — don't just say 'whatever you give'.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What if I don't know the answer to a question?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: You don't have to know everything. It's okay to say 'That's a great question — let me think for a moment.' You ought to be honest rather than guessing.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Should I ask them questions at the end?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: You should absolutely ask two or three good questions. It shows you're genuinely interested. Have you thought about asking about the team culture or growth opportunities?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Good idea! Do I need to carry anything?

dialogue.speaker: Father
dialogue.text: You must carry extra copies of your CV and your certificates. You should also carry a notepad and pen — it looks professional.

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake — these are mixed errors from all of Unit 7.

sentences.wrong: You don't have to smoke in the hospital — it is strictly forbidden.
sentences.correct: You mustn't smoke in the hospital — it is strictly forbidden.
sentences.explanation: STRICTLY FORBIDDEN = MUSTN'T. DON'T HAVE TO means not necessary, not forbidden.

sentences.wrong: She have to work late yesterday because of the deadline.
sentences.correct: She had to work late yesterday because of the deadline.
sentences.explanation: Past obligation: HAD TO. Also, 'she' uses 'had', not 'have'.

sentences.wrong: Have you thought about to change your study schedule?
sentences.correct: Have you thought about changing your study schedule?
sentences.explanation: After 'Have you thought about', use the -ing form.

content.scenario: You are helping your colleague write an office safety notice. One rule says that all employees are strictly forbidden from bringing food into the server room. How do you write this rule?
options: Employees don't have to bring food into the server room.
Employees should not bring food into the server room.
Employees mustn't bring food or drink into the server room.
Employees don't need to bring food into the server room.
content.correct: Employees mustn't bring food or drink into the server room.
content.explanation: Strictly forbidden = MUSTN'T. Should not is possible but weaker. The other options don't express prohibition.

content.instruction: Unit 7 Final Speaking Task 1: Think about a place with many rules — an airport, a hospital, a temple, a school exam hall, or a government office. Explain all the rules using must, mustn't, have to, and don't have to.
content.prompt: Describe the rules at a public place in India. Give at least 6 rules using the obligation language from Unit 7.
content.example_response: I want to talk about the rules at Chennai airport, which I've been to a few times. When you arrive at the airport, you must show your boarding pass and ID at the entrance — they check everyone. You mustn't carry sharp objects, liquids over 100ml, or any flammable items in your hand luggage — that's a strict aviation security rule. You have to remove your shoes, belt, and laptop from your bag when going through the security scanner. After security, you don't have to rush — you have time to browse the shops or sit and wait. But you must always listen to the gate announcements — boarding can sometimes start early. You mustn't leave your bags unattended at any point — unattended luggage will be removed by security. On the plane, you must switch your phone to flight mode or switch it off completely. You don't have to do anything else especially — just sit back and enjoy the flight!

content.instruction: Unit 7 Final Speaking Task 2: Give advice to someone starting their first job in India — a young person of 22 who has never worked before. What should they know? What should they do and not do?
content.prompt: Give comprehensive advice to a new graduate starting their first job. Use all the advice language from Unit 7.
content.example_response: Welcome to the working world! Starting your first job is exciting and a little scary, so let me give you some advice. First, you must always be punctual — in most Indian offices, coming late regularly is taken very seriously, especially in your first few months. You should dress professionally, even if the company has a casual dress code — it's better to be slightly over-dressed than under-dressed in the beginning. You mustn't speak negatively about your colleagues, manager, or the company on social media — even if you think no one is watching, people usually are. If I were you, I would focus on learning and asking good questions rather than trying to show off what you know. You don't have to socialise with everyone straight away — take your time to understand the office culture first. You should take notes in meetings — it shows you are serious. And you ought to build a good relationship with your manager — have regular check-ins and ask for feedback. Don't be afraid to ask for help — everyone was new once!

content.instruction: Unit 7 comprehensive gap fill. Choose from: must, mustn't, have to, don't have to, need to, needn't, should, shouldn't, ought to.

sentences.text: The fire exit ___ be kept clear at all times. (mandatory — safety)
You ___ worry about the interview — you're very well prepared. (not necessary)
I ___ renew my driving licence — it expired last month. (necessity)
The event is free — you ___ pay for a ticket. (not required)
You ___ share your banking passwords with anyone. (prohibited)
You ___ see a dentist twice a year for a check-up. (advice)

content.title: Unit 7 COMPLETE — Obligations, rules & advice
key_points: MUST = strong official obligation
MUSTN'T = prohibition (not allowed)
HAVE TO / HAS TO = external obligation
DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary
NEED TO / NEEDN'T = necessity / not necessary
SHOULD / SHOULDN'T = advice
IF I WERE YOU, I WOULD = strong personal advice
WHY DON'T YOU / WHAT ABOUT + -ing = suggestion
OUGHT TO = formal advice
